speaker: interviewer
text: Tema, you were telling us that you were separated from your parents and your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the town like where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- well, the one that they sent me to was this-- Bodzentyn, which was a small little town. My parents were sent to Starachowice, which was not far from that town. And-- but later when I found out where they were, I went there. And that was the town, actually, where they had the labor camp, that I was taken into that labor camp. That was in Starachowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see, how long were you in the town before you went to where your parents were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it's-- it's real hard for me to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] let's see, '41, '42. Oh, well, we went through-- I went through a winter there, I'm sure. So that must have been like the next spring-- in the summertime, I was taken to the camp. And my [INAUDIBLE]-- my parents were taken away a day before, and I didn't know where they were taking them. And we didn't know what's going to happen to my sister and myself. But then, the next morning, about 5:00 in the morning, 6:00 in the morning, we heard Juden raus, Juden raus, so we all had to go, like, to the marketplace. And that's when we saw already what went on-- the-- the Gestapo, and-- and they were-- they were selecting people who was on the train right away that-- which needs to be killed. And the younger people, they left to take to camp to work for them. And there were-- there were-- there were terrible, terrible scenes at the time because they made mothers leave their children and go-- go to work, and the-- the-- the children were plain just thrown in on the train, lay there And whoever had a-- a bandage on, or-- or I had a friend that had a-- a broken leg and he was walking on with a crutch, and they killed him immediately. Because he was unable to work, so he was not good for them. They weren't going to save him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people who were killed immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I saw quite a few times people killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, one was killed because, when they took us into this camp, they announced to give up everything. Because if they'll find anything on anybody, they'll be killed-- killed immediately. And my friend's older brother, unknowingly he had a few zlotys in his vest little pocket that he didn't even know. He found it. He checked him, of all the people. He found it and killed him right in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea of where the people were going who were taken away on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time, no, we had no idea. When we stopped sometimes at the station, we would try to ask somebody, and-- and they didn't know where the train was going.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in one of these selection processes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were you in selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the concentration camps, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, here. You said at the marketplace, the Gestapos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, of course. When they selected, I was selected to go to the factory, to the Hermann Goering ammunition factory. And my sister, also. So we were both there and then we were taken, of course, to that camp that they made ready for us, which was a barracks, you know, put together from thin plywood or whatever, and there were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] three rows of-- what do you call-- bunks, you know, on three different levels.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so and we-- I had the-- the one right in the corner. And in the wintertime, the frost was inside the wood, you know? But-- and we also didn't have anything to cover ourselves with because, right away, when the Germans started the war with Russia, they ordered every Jew to give up every fur they have, even collars from little children's coats, because they needed it to-- to send it to the front or something, for their soldiers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had a coat at the time that was lined with fur, and I didn't know what to do. And I figured, well, they can't see the fur. And everybody said, but you'll pay with your life for it, if they'll stop you and they'll see it. So in-- in order not to give them the whole thing, I took out the fur and I was left only with the fabric of that coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister lay down next to me on that bunk that didn't have even straw, not speaking of a cover or a pillow, just bare wood. She laid down next to me and we covered ourselves, both of us, with that fabric from that coat. That's all we had. What about the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- the food was-- you know, just-- actually just to survive. Just to survive, that's all. And-- and-- and work, you know. Every-- every morning we were-- we were walked in the middle of the street and forced to go to this ammunition factory. And I remember that the lights use to flicker in people's windows that lived there, and-- because they were getting ready to go to work. They got up in their homes and got ready to go to work. And I'll never forget, I thought to myself, [CRYING] if I could change to a broom, to a broom, to just be somewhere in a warm corner. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the factory, we had to work quite hard. We got some soup, you know, which was mostly water with a few potatoes, and a piece of bread, and that was for the day. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after work, they marched us back to camp. The same thing, and it was-- if it was snowing or slippery, we had a lot of people that didn't even have shoes. They were marched barefoot, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were very few that had much clothes, because when they send me out from Plock, I remember that we were not allowed to take any packages at all. So some people would put on their winter coat on top of a spring coat, or layers of clothes. If the Germans would see it, they would absolutely take away the winter coat, not the spring coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother put a towel around her neck, just like a scarf, just to have something, you know? If you wash your hands or-- because we didn't have anything. A German pulled it off her neck. He saw it and he pulled it off her neck. Were there German guards at this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: German guards, and later there were Ukrainians. Later on, they were Ukrainian guards. And also, with the Ukrainian guards, see, the Germans would not do anything with the Jewish girls. They were not allowed. But they certainly sent in Ukrainians to do it. I slept with my sister in one bunker, and we thought that would help. The-- the cries and the screams were unbelievable during the night in those, you know, camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anyone that you could go to for help?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were all in the same situation. We were all in the same situation. There was nobody to go for help. We were closed up in that camp, and marched to work and marched from work. That's all. And the only thing that was happening if somebody would escape-- of course they had a list of all the people in the camps. And if somebody would escape, they would take down five of the healthiest young boys [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] and brought the whole camp down to that place. They put up the headlights on a car so we could see the boys being killed, because one escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they kill the boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoot them. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And the-- the camp director, the commandant, after each time he did it, he would go up there and say, this is what's going to happen if you escape. But if you stay, and you don't escape, I'll be like a father to you. Two days later, he came with his company, and for amusement, he killed out 50 people during [INAUDIBLE] during the night. And when we got up to go to work, we were actually walking in the puddles of blood because it was raining that night, too. And we had-- before they were-- they took away the bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Althof. Althof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it's A-- A-L-T-H-O-- either one or double F.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was the commander of-- of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the commandant, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The commandant. And what was the camp called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh God. It was called Strzelnica. Strzelnic in Poland means to shoot. And that was a place where they were teaching, I think, Polish soldiers, you know, shooting from the rifles or whatever. So they called it Strzelnica. S-T-R-Z-E-L-N-I-C-A. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And that was in Starachowice. I think I mentioned, that's the town that they took my parents and my sister to after they separated me from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that town's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Which one? Starachowice? Sure. S-T-A-R-A-C-H-O-W-I-C-E. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Was there any sort of medical care there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where, at the camp? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No care at all. Couldn't go to work, they were killed immediately. There was no-- no time for that, no. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me. And the women-- the women, of course were given something. I don't know, either something that they add to the soups, or-- because I don't remember getting any pills, so it had to be in a form that they put into the soups that we were getting that no woman menstruated in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you and your sister there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister-- well, I was the first one that came down with typhoid fever. But the reason I survived, I think, was that was this commandant was sick himself away in a hospital. But from the time he came back, there were no survivors. Once somebody got sick, they were killed. He even fooled us. There was a barrack, number five, that, if we're sick and we can't go to work, and if we're going to that barrack, he'll see to it that we get help, and get well, and be able to go back to work. Nobody survived there more than two days. He came and killed them at night. Then everybody was afraid to go to barrack five.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: When did I escape? I escaped in the fall of, I think, '43. I think '43. Mm-hm. Yeah, and that was-- we were a small group, going back-- not on our shift, so we were about, boy, I would say 20 people. My sister, I had a chance to send out to-- see, she was very sick with-- with diarrhea and-- very, very sick. So-- and this man offered to take her out and possibly take her to a doctor, bring her back to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a foreman at this-- at this factory where we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a Christian man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he took her out, but then his wife would not allow him to keep her overnight there because she was afraid that if the Germans would find out, they would kill the whole family. Which in a way, I cannot blame her for it. But anyhow, he came back once to the-- I did not go back to camp after-- it's-- I-- I can't, you know, to make you understand it. When he came and he killed, let's say, 50 people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that was the time to escape because they put you on the list of the killed and nobody had to be killed for you when you escaped. And that it was, Jews were watching each other. And when my sister was sick, there were people that came over to me and he said, look, you give your life for your sister. I'm not willing to. Then they came, they said, now it's the time. Today or never. Because we'll put you on the list of the dead and that's it, and nobody will pay for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the day when I-- I had to escape. And that's why I walked out from the factory with the small group. and I had a friend from my hometown, from Plock, and he said, Tema, your life is at stake, so you will be nervous. But when I tell you go, and he was-- I was like in the last row of the women, he was in the first row of the men, directly behind me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, when I say go, you turn left when we all turn right. And that's what I did, not even looking that maybe I'm doing it next to a German. But he said go, and I went. And then I-- I had somebody that took me to this village that he knew about, this man, because-- it's a long story. I was supposed to take a family's young child-- a little boy. They knew somebody in the village that was willing to save me with the boy, because they did it in order to save their child.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the day before he was going to come for me and that baby, they-- they had this thing that we-- we all went to the-- to the camp. So it was too late. So when my sister got sick, then I-- I asked this man, whether was it possible that I am the one that was going to be there with the child and so on, would it be possible for him to help us? And he agreed. How were you dressed when you left the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the clothes that I had. We didn't have uniforms, if that's what you-- what you meant, no. In whatever we had, that's what we wore, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you wearing a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the camp. It wasn't needed. There were only Jews in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you were able to leave the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leave on my own?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you escaped.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that I escaped, nobody saw it. That's how I escaped. That the group went-- made a left-- a right turn going to the camp, and I, by myself, turned away to the left. And when they were a little distance away, I ran across the street, and there were bushes, and I was hiding in the bushes, and then I walked out, you know, to the sidewalk to see whether I would see somebody.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody was waiting for his date. And I said, oh, your date is late, too. He said yes, would you mind waiting together? Well, his date came, but my date never came. So I was left there in the bushes. But a-- they noticed that somebody was missing in that row. And the Ukrainians came looking, but at that time, I was walking with this man on the sidewalk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's how I escaped. That's how I got to this village later and spent there till the end of the war, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: What village did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: What what?

speaker: interviewer
text: What village did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bronkowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-R-O-N-K-O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go when you arrived in the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: To this man by the name [? Novak ?] that was going to find a place for me with this little child to save him. So I-- I stayed with-- with that family. When somebody knocked on the door, they put me into the wardrobe. There were-- you know, in Europe, they-- you didn't have closets like you have here. You have standing wardrobes, you know. So they put me into there and closed the door. And I stayed there until-- until the people left. Then finally he told them that he has a cousin that escaped from the Germans because they're looking for her, and he told her it's OK to come to him. So from that day on, I didn't have to hide. I was like in the open and known as a cousin of the family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I was-- I was once outside, and the-- and the-- the people, the villagers, were working in the fields, and a man came running and he said to me, there are two Jews coming out from the forest here. I want you to talk to them and make them wait, because I'm running home for a gun. Well, I knew that he was going to kill them. So when I saw them coming, I could not stop to tell them, don't go, he's going to kill you. Because that means, why would I save Jews? I must be Jewish myself. A stranger in the village, and-- and warning the Jews not to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in passing by, I said, run back to the forest because they came out of the forest. I said, run back to the forest, and I kept going. And this man came on the bicycle-- the one with the gun-- came on the bicycle, and they-- they didn't hear me right. And they starting asking, what, what? But I couldn't stop with them. I could not stop to talk to them. So, not knowing me, what they did to me, they said to the man, we will not go with you because you're going to kill us. I didn't tell them. I knew what they were going to do. There was no time to tell them. I just told them, run back to the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from that day on, it started. Finally, towards the end of the war, he kept quiet, he went to the underground, to the AK, which is [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland, and he told them that he's suspicious of me. And they would come and interview me and ask me, and couldn't prove anything. But finally he decided that if I-- if he doesn't do something for-- with me, if I am Jewish, then if the Russians come in, I'll tell them, and they'll kill him. So he went to Gestapo. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the Gestapo came and arrested me. And at the time, they wouldn't tell me why. They took some smugglers that were in the village, and they took us all to this Starachowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: What name were you using while you were living with [? Novak? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was using Wladyslawa Smiechowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please? Wladyslawa is W-L-A-D-Y-S-L-A-W-A. [LAUGHS] Smiechowska is S-M-I-E-C-H-O-W-S-K-A. And you were hiding your identity with false papers, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at the time, I didn't have the papers. I tore them up in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just used the Polish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: To be with my sister, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't really have any papers at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the Gestapo say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they took me to prison in Starachowice. And they-- and they interrogated me. It was a small room-- a very small room with a big desk and a big dog lying on the floor-- and they started to interrogate me in German because this man told them that I was teaching the Polish children, and that I was teaching them also German. And I was afraid because it was enough one word, you know, spoken incorrectly, that I would pay with my life right away. And I said no, I only know a little German, what I was taught in school. And other than that, I would need a translator. I don't understand you.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the meantime, I understood everything what they were talking between them. And they were saying, should we play around any longer, or should we tell her why we arrested her? Till finally they came out and they told me, you're Jewish, aren't you? Well, that was after a while of me being in the village and being very much aware of what-- everything, you know. And occasionally of even going to church with the people from the village. And I jumped up and I crossed myself and I said, I am Jewish? How dare you? Who told you, you know? I don't think I could-- I would have the guts anymore now, but then, somehow, I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they looked at each other. They didn't-- they didn't realize what's going on. And then they-- they took me back to the cell and again interrogated me and again interrogated. And once, on the way back from interrogation, I was going to my cell, and a man was sitting there-- a German-- and he said oh, Smiechowska, aus Juden. And he thought he'll catch me. When he'll say aus Juden, I will turn around and try to defend myself, which means I do speak German.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say to you in German? Could you translate what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aus-- aus Juden, means that you come from Jews. You're Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I pretended that I didn't even hear him, and I went. And this was right after Christmas, so there was-- they could look in through the-- what do you call that hole in the doors that you can look in and out? What do you call those?

speaker: interviewer
text: Peephole.

speaker: interviewee
text: Peepholes. There was a peephole into the cell, and I didn't know, but they were watching me. And I was singing the Christmas songs with the other women that were arrested. And they could not-- not that they had to prove much, so another Polish woman would be killed, so what? Instead of leaving a Jew alive. But I don't know why that they just didn't do it. And they took me out. The-- the tricks that they use. They took me out to the-- to the office, and they said they would like me to clean it up. Well, I cleaned it up. I moved everything and I dusted everything and I washed the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said no, she's not Jewish. Out of the question. A Jewish girl would not know how to clean. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They told me to scrub the hallway in prison, and I said, fine. I need a brush, and I need soap, and I need hot water, you know? And they-- they watched me. They watched every move which I didn't know. At one time, a German stopped me in the hallway in prison, and he started speaking Polish to me. And he said, yes, he went to school in Poland, he knows Polish, and I don't have to be afraid of him. He'll-- he'll help me. I should just tell him what's going on. Why am I here, and so on, and so forth? Just tell him, that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very sweet. Like Althof said, I'm your father. And I said all you can do for me is let them check out where I came from and let-- and let me go. There's nothing else I have to say. Another time, a German took me, but in the evening, took me to a separate room. And he was going to interrogate me there, just the two of us. And all of a sudden, I see he walked over and he locked the door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I started yelling to open the door, and I started banging on the door. Help, open the door. Open the door. And everything led them to believe that it's impossible, because I was offered help so many times and didn't take it. And I had the guts to knock on the door, to open the door for me, when I was with the German. Anyhow, they let me go. They freed me-- I think it was about the 12th of January, right after Christmas. And the 16th of January, the Russians came in and I was freed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was liberated by the-- by the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And then after the war, I went back to my hometown to see who survived and--
